title: LOLBin Execution from Office
briefing: Sysmon on $TARGET shows a signed Windows binary (rundll32 / regsvr32 / mshta / certutil) launched by an Office application and reaching out to a remote URL. Attackers abuse these living-off-the-land binaries to run code while blending in with trusted, signed tooling. Identify the binary, the parent, and the remote host.
dashboard_hint: Discover -> rule.id: 100142 (LOLBin). Read data.win.eventdata.image + commandLine + parentImage (WINWORD.EXE).

questions.explain: A signed system binary spawned by Office and calling out to a URL is LOLBin proxy execution.

questions.prompt: What makes the execution suspicious rather than normal?
options: An Office app (WINWORD) is the parent process
It came from the domain controller
questions.answer: An Office app (WINWORD) is the parent process
questions.explain: rundll32/regsvr32/etc launched by WINWORD.EXE with a network arg is a macro/payload pattern, not admin use.

questions.prompt: What remote host did the binary contact (from the command line)?
questions.explain: The LOLBin command line references $SRC1 as the remote payload/C2 host.
T1218 System Binary Proxy Execution (Signed Binary Proxy Execution).

options: Deploy ASR rules blocking Office child processes
Nothing — these binaries are signed by Microsoft
answer: Deploy ASR rules blocking Office child processes
questions.explain: Signed does not mean safe. Contain, block the remote, chase the phishing doc, and stop Office spawning children via ASR.
